old_claim.note: The previous GPS graph represented generic satellite technology but omitted the coordinated multi-satellite architecture documented for NAVSTAR.

new_claim.note: NAVSTAR's worldwide positioning architecture requires a coordinated constellation of orbiting satellites to provide the geometry and continuous ranging coverage unavailable from one spacecraft.

source_shape.source_locator: Report pp. 1-3, System Description and Space Segment: specifies a constellation of orbiting satellites arranged to provide worldwide navigation coverage and continuous broadcasts.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The contemporaneous program report defines NAVSTAR's space segment as a coordinated satellite constellation rather than a single spacecraft.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Generic satellite technology captures the platform, but the GPS claim also depends on multi-satellite geometry and continuous coverage, so the constellation architecture needs its own direct edge.

ontology_before: GPS depended on generic satellite technology without explicitly representing its coordinated multi-satellite space-segment architecture.
ontology_after: GPS requires both the satellite platform and the constellation-level arrangement that supplies worldwide ranging geometry.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-gps-navstar-logistics-boundary: satellite_constellations remains required for gps.
would_reject_if: The GPS node is rescoped to a single-spacecraft experiment that does not claim continuous worldwide positioning.
Primary architecture evidence shows that the scoped NAVSTAR design did not require coordinated signals from multiple satellites.
short_reason: NAVSTAR's positioning geometry and worldwide coverage require a coordinated satellite constellation.
